lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: O diable!

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: O seigneur! le jour est perdu, tout est perdu!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Mort de ma vie! all is confounded, all!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Reproach and everlasting shame

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Sits mocking in our plumes. O méchante fortune!

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Do not run away. A short alarum.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Why, all our ranks are broke.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: O perdurable shame! let’s stab ourselves.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Be these the wretches that we play’d at dice for?

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: Is this the king we sent to for his ransom?

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Shame and eternal shame, nothing but shame!

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Let’s die in honour: once more back again;

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: And he that will not follow Bourbon now,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Let him go hence, and with his cap in hand,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Like a base pandar, hold the chamber-door

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Whilst by a slave, no gentler than my dog,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: His fairest daughter is contaminated.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Disorder, that hath spoil’d us, friend us now!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Let us on heaps go offer up our lives.

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: We are enough yet living in the field

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: To smother up the English in our throngs,

lines.speaker: ORLEANS
lines.text: If any order might be thought upon.

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: The devil take order now! I’ll to the throng;

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Let life be short; else shame will be too long.

stage_directions.text: Enter Constable, Orleans, Bourbon, Dauphin, and Rambures.

glosses.definition: French: 'O devil' — the Constable's cry as the French collapse

glosses.phrase: O seigneur! le jour est perdu, tout est perdu
glosses.definition: French: 'O lord! the day is lost, all is lost'
French: 'Death of my life' (already glossed in 3.5)
mocking shame rides in the plumes of our helmets

glosses.phrase: wretches that we play’d at dice for
glosses.definition: the English we gambled prisoner-rights over last night — recall 3.7 and 4 Chorus
Bourbon's rallying cry — fall back into the battle to die well

glosses.phrase: Disorder, that hath spoil’d us, friend us now
glosses.definition: disorder ruined us — let chaos help us in our last charge

glosses.phrase: on heaps go offer up our lives
glosses.definition: in disorganized masses, sacrifice our lives

glosses.phrase: smother up the English in our throngs
glosses.definition: overwhelm the English with our sheer numbers
if we live long, our shame will last longer

annotations.title: The French collapse in twenty lines
annotations.body: Scene 4.5 is the shortest scene in the play. The French nobles — the same Dauphin, Bourbon, Orleans, and Constable whose horse-boasts filled 3.7 — now cry out 'O diable!' and 'O seigneur! le jour est perdu, tout est perdu!' The brevity is the point. Shakespeare does not stage a French defeat scene of equal weight to any English triumph scene. Instead, he gives us twenty-three lines of panic, and then moves to 4.6, where Henry names York's death. The structural effect is fast and somewhat cruel: 3.7's fifty-line horse-bragging is paid off with 4.5's twenty-line collapse, and the ratio itself enacts the play's judgment. Bourbon's final line — 'The devil take order now! I'll to the throng; / Let life be short; else shame will be too long' — is the only dignified note. The rest is confusion. This is structural contempt rendered as pacing.
sources: Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: What is notable about the length of 4.5?
options: It is the longest scene in Act IV
It is the shortest scene in the play — 23 lines; the brevity is the point: 3.7's long horse-bragging scene is paid off with a compressed collapse, the ratio enacting the play's judgment
It has been cut in most modern productions
